name: Popover
support: The global attribute is broadly interoperable; the related API surface continues to gain refinements.
summary: Build top-layer menus, teaching bubbles, and non-modal floating panels without owning z-index, outside-click, or escape-key behavior yourself.
solves: Layered UI that needs light dismiss and top-layer placement, but does not need to make the rest of the page inert.
demo: A command tray that opens with one attribute and can be dismissed by clicking outside or pressing Esc.
usage: Use for small transient surfaces: menus, quick actions, hints, onboarding notes, and tool palettes. Use dialog when the task must block the page.
pitfalls: Popover does not provide dialog semantics by itself.
Choose auto, hint, or manual based on whether light dismiss and one-at-a-time behavior are desired.
Positioning still needs CSS; the top layer only solves stacking.
accessibility: Use meaningful buttons as invokers.
Add semantic roles only when the content actually behaves like a menu, tooltip, or note.
Do not put required workflow steps in a light-dismissible popover.

name: Invoker Commands
support: Newly available across the core Baseline browser set since late 2025.
summary: Let buttons declaratively command popovers, dialogs, and custom command handlers without waiting for app JavaScript to wire click listeners.
solves: HTML-first controls for common show, hide, toggle, and close behaviors.
demo: A toolbar where buttons control a popover and a dialog through command attributes rather than click handlers.
usage: Use as progressive enhancement for built-in interactive elements, especially where initial interactivity matters before framework hydration.
pitfalls: Only buttons are invokers.
Built-in commands are intentionally limited.
Framework abstractions may need updates before they pass through the attributes cleanly.
accessibility: Keep the invoker a real button.
Use visible labels; do not rely on icon-only commands.
Provide JS fallback only when your support target needs it.

name: Dialog requestClose
support: The dialog element is mature; requestClose is newly available and gives cancellable close behavior.
summary: Use native modal behavior, return values, form method=dialog, and cancellable close requests without recreating focus management.
solves: Confirmation flows and modal forms that need platform-level focus handling plus an explicit result.
demo: A shipping confirmation dialog that returns the selected action and can intercept risky close attempts.
usage: Use for modal tasks, confirmations, and short forms. Pair with form method=dialog for zero-boilerplate button return values.
pitfalls: showModal() is imperative; you still need a tiny trigger unless using invoker commands.
requestClose() can be cancelled, close() cannot.
Backdrop click behavior depends on newer closedby support and should be treated as an enhancement.
accessibility: Give every dialog a visible heading.
Provide an obvious close or cancel path.
Avoid nesting complex app navigation inside a modal.

name: Exclusive details
support: Supported in modern browsers as the name attribute on grouped details elements.
summary: Turn a group of details elements into a native exclusive accordion by giving them the same name.
solves: FAQ, settings, and comparison sections where only one disclosure should be open at a time.
demo: A delivery options accordion with one open panel and no JavaScript state management.
usage: Use when the content is genuinely disclosure content, not tabs or app navigation.
pitfalls: details is not a tab widget.
Only use exclusive behavior when closing the previous panel will not cause data loss.
Custom summary styling must keep focus and expanded state visible.
accessibility: Keep summary text clear and action-oriented.
Do not remove default keyboard behavior.
Avoid putting large forms inside summary.

name: Search element
support: The search element is available across modern browsers and maps to a search landmark.
summary: Represent site, page, or app search with a dedicated landmark instead of repeating role=search on generic containers.
solves: Search UI semantics for screen readers and document structure.
syntax:
<search>
  <form action="/search">
    <label for="q">Search docs</label>
    <input id="q" name="q" type="search">
  </form>
</search>
demo: A feature filter that uses search semantics and keeps results outside the search landmark.
usage: Use around the controls that perform search or filtering, not around the search results.
pitfalls: The element does not implement search logic.
Results should remain in the main content, not inside search.
Do not nest multiple search landmarks without clear labels.
accessibility: Keep visible labels for fields.
Name multiple search landmarks with aria-label.
Use type=search for query fields.

name: Declarative Shadow DOM
support: Baseline newly available since 2024 with the standardized shadowrootmode attribute.
summary: Ship scoped component markup from the server by declaring a shadow root directly in HTML.
solves: Server-rendered Web Components that need style encapsulation before JavaScript upgrades.
demo: An iframe-parsed server-rendered profile badge whose shadow styles do not leak into the page.
usage: Use when a component needs SSR, streaming, or static HTML output but still benefits from shadow DOM boundaries.
pitfalls: The shadowrootmode template is parser-only; setting it later with innerHTML does not attach a shadow root.
Older examples may use the removed shadowroot attribute.
Hydrating custom elements must check for an existing shadow root.
accessibility: Keep light DOM content meaningful before upgrade.
Do not hide essential text inside inaccessible custom control internals.
Use slots deliberately so reading order remains sensible.

name: Plaintext-only editing
support: Baseline newly available since March 2025.
summary: Make an element editable while forcing pasted and edited content to remain plain text.
solves: Comment boxes, command inputs, note fields, and token editors that need contenteditable ergonomics without rich-text paste cleanup.
demo: A release-note scratchpad that shows the raw HTML stays plain while the editable surface remains flexible.
usage: Use when textarea is too restrictive but rich content would create sanitization and formatting problems.
pitfalls: It is not a complete form control by itself.
You still need validation, persistence, and submission logic.
Line breaks and whitespace need product-specific handling.
accessibility: Give the editable region a label.
Expose role=textbox and aria-multiline when appropriate.
Preserve focus styling.

name: showPicker
support: Available across modern browsers for input pickers since 2022, with select.showPicker still more limited.
summary: Open native date, color, file, and related browser pickers from your own visible control without rebuilding the picker UI.
solves: Design-friendly trigger buttons that still use platform-native input pickers.
demo: A travel form with custom trigger buttons that open real browser pickers.
usage: Use when your layout needs a separate trigger but the actual picker should remain native.
pitfalls: The method must usually run from a user activation such as a click.
Support varies by input type and platform.
Do not fake picker contents; let the browser own that part.
accessibility: Keep the real input in the tab order unless there is a strong reason not to.
Connect labels to inputs.
Mirror chosen values in readable text when using compact controls.

name: inert
support: Widely available in the modern core browser set.
summary: Mark a subtree as non-interactive so it cannot be clicked, focused, selected, or reached by assistive technology.
solves: Disabled page regions behind overlays, preview panes, inactive steps, and temporary UI locks.
demo: A settings workspace where the background becomes truly unavailable while a review layer is active.
usage: Use when a whole region should be unavailable, not merely visually dimmed.
pitfalls: inert removes interactivity from descendants, including links and form controls.
Do not inert content that remains necessary to finish the task.
Visual dimming is separate; add CSS to make the state obvious.
accessibility: Pair inert with a clear active region.
Move focus intentionally when enabling an overlay.
Restore focus when leaving the inert state.

name: Customizable select
support: Experimental and not Baseline; useful to learn now as a progressive enhancement.
summary: Style native select internals with HTML and CSS hooks such as button, selectedcontent, ::picker(select), and appearance: base-select.
solves: Rich option displays that preserve native select behavior instead of replacing it with ARIA-heavy custom widgets.
demo: A shipping method picker whose selected value and dropdown rows can carry structured content.
usage: Use experimentally or behind progressive enhancement checks; classic select fallback remains important.
pitfalls: Not Baseline yet.
Framework hydration may disagree with browser-managed selectedcontent cloning.
Dynamic option updates may not automatically re-clone selectedcontent.
accessibility: Do not use it for split buttons or unrelated composite controls.
Keep option text meaningful without decoration.
Confirm keyboard behavior in every target browser.

name: hidden until found
support: Available in several modern browsers but not Baseline.
summary: Keep content collapsed while allowing find-in-page and URL fragments to reveal it automatically.
solves: Large reference pages, archived sections, and dense docs where hidden content should still be discoverable.
demo: A compressed changelog section that reveals itself when navigated to by fragment.
usage: Use for non-critical collapsed content that still needs browser search and deep-link discovery.
pitfalls: Not all browsers reveal until-found content.
The element still participates in layout with containment behavior in supporting browsers.
Do not hide primary navigation or required form fields this way.
accessibility: Provide a normal visible path to reveal the same content.
Use stable fragment ids.
Avoid making search the only way to find important information.

name: Responsive images
support: The picture element, srcset, and sizes are broadly interoperable; final selection depends on media queries, device pixel ratio, image type support, and layout.
summary: Let the browser choose the right image candidate for viewport, density, and format instead of shipping one oversized asset everywhere.
solves: Art-directed hero images, dense product grids, and editorial media where layout and network cost change across breakpoints.
demo: An editorial media slot that documents which candidate the browser is allowed to pick at each layout size.
usage: Use width descriptors with sizes for flexible layouts. Use picture when the crop or format should change, not just the resolution.
pitfalls: Wrong sizes values can make the browser choose a much larger image than needed.
picture still needs an img fallback with useful alt text.
Do not use art direction to hide important content on small screens.
accessibility: Keep alt text on the img element.
Make every art-directed crop preserve the same meaning.
Avoid text baked into responsive images when real text can be used.

name: Native lazy loading
support: Native lazy loading is widely available for images and iframes; exact load distance is intentionally browser-controlled.
summary: Defer below-the-fold images and iframes with one attribute instead of shipping scroll observers for every asset.
solves: Long content pages, galleries, embeds, and docs where non-critical media should not compete with above-the-fold content.
demo: A gallery queue that separates eager hero media from lazy supporting media.
usage: Use lazy for offscreen media. Keep critical hero images eager and pair them with explicit width and height to avoid layout shift.
pitfalls: Lazy loading above-the-fold images can hurt perceived performance.
The browser decides when a lazy resource is close enough to load.
Always reserve dimensions so unloaded media does not shift content.
accessibility: Keep alt text and iframe titles meaningful.
Do not lazy-load content that is required before the user can understand the page.
Avoid replacing lazy media with inaccessible placeholders.

name: Fetch priority
support: The fetchpriority attribute is Baseline newly available; browsers treat it as a priority hint rather than a hard scheduling command.
summary: Tell the browser which otherwise-similar resources deserve higher or lower fetch priority directly in markup.
solves: Hero images, late-discovered preloads, and low-value decorative resources that need better relative ordering.
demo: A launch page resource board that marks the hero image high and decorative assets low.
usage: Use sparingly for resources whose importance the browser cannot infer. Prefer fixing discovery order before adding priority hints.
pitfalls: fetchpriority does not make a hidden or late-discovered resource magically early.
Too many high-priority resources cancel out the signal.
Priority hints are browser scheduling hints, not guaranteed ordering.
accessibility: Performance hints must not change source order or reading order.
Do not prioritize decorative media over meaningful content.
Keep fallback content and alt text independent of priority.

name: Preload links
support: rel=preload is widely available for declaring high-confidence resources early in the document head.
summary: Start fetching critical fonts, images, or scripts before the browser would otherwise discover them.
solves: Critical resources referenced from CSS, late template chunks, and above-the-fold assets that should arrive earlier.
syntax:
<link rel="preload" href="/fonts/plex.woff2" as="font" type="font/woff2" crossorigin>
<link rel="preload" href="/hero.avif" as="image" imagesrcset="hero.avif 1200w, hero@2x.avif 2400w" imagesizes="100vw">
demo: A checkout page head plan that distinguishes preload candidates from ordinary assets.
usage: Use only for resources needed by the current navigation. Include the correct as, type, and crossorigin values.
pitfalls: Preloading unused resources wastes bandwidth.
Wrong as or crossorigin values can trigger duplicate downloads.
Do not preload everything; the browser already has a scheduler.
accessibility: Preload should improve delivery without changing semantic order.
Do not hide meaningful fallback text while waiting for preloaded assets.
Prioritize resources that help users read or operate the page.

name: modulepreload
support: modulepreload is widely available in modern browsers for warming JavaScript module graphs.
summary: Preload ES modules into the module map so app entrypoints and their dependencies are ready sooner.
solves: Module-based apps where the parser can see an entrypoint but the dependency graph would otherwise be discovered step by step.
demo: A module graph warmup plan that shows which imports should be available before hydration starts.
usage: Use for important module entrypoints or chunks you know the current page will execute.
pitfalls: modulepreload is not a replacement for code splitting.
Preloading stale or route-only chunks can waste bandwidth.
Cross-origin modules need correct CORS behavior.
accessibility: Module warmup should not be required for core readable content.
Keep server-rendered content meaningful before modules execute.
Avoid blocking essential controls on non-critical modules.

name: blocking=render
support: The blocking attribute is useful to understand but should be treated as a targeted enhancement while support matures.
summary: Explicitly make a dynamically inserted script, style, or stylesheet block rendering until it is ready.
solves: Small critical head resources that are injected dynamically but still must apply before first paint.
syntax:
<script async blocking="render" src="/critical-theme.js"></script>
<link rel="stylesheet" blocking="render" href="/critical.css">
demo: A first-paint checklist that separates render-blocking theme code from non-critical enhancements.
usage: Use only for small, truly critical resources. Most pages should prefer normal parser-discovered stylesheets and avoid extra render blocking.
pitfalls: Render blocking delays first paint.
Only head resources can affect render blocking.
Large scripts or stylesheets can make the page feel frozen.
accessibility: Do not block rendering on decorative-only resources.
Keep initial content understandable if the enhancement fails.
Avoid blocking controls needed for emergency or account flows.

name: Import maps
support: Import maps have been broadly available across modern browsers since March 2023.
summary: Map bare JavaScript module specifiers to URLs directly in HTML without a bundler rewrite.
solves: Small apps, prototypes, design systems, and server-rendered pages that want readable module imports in the browser.
demo: A design-system import map that routes stable package names to versioned browser modules.
usage: Define import maps before the module scripts that rely on them. Keep maps small and cache module URLs deliberately.
pitfalls: Import maps are document-scoped.
They must be parsed before dependent modules load.
Large maps can become deployment configuration that needs version control.
accessibility: Do not make basic content depend on a client-only module map.
Keep no-JS rendering meaningful.
Use module errors that fail visibly during development.

name: Speculation rules
support: Speculation rules are not Baseline; support is strongest in Chromium-derived browsers and should be used as progressive enhancement.
summary: Declare likely future navigations so the browser can prefetch or prerender whole documents.
solves: Multi-page apps, docs, ecommerce flows, and dashboards where the next navigation is predictable.
demo: A docs navigation plan that prefetches likely next pages but avoids state-changing routes.
usage: Use on safe GET navigations. Start with conservative prefetch rules before trying prerender.
pitfalls: Do not speculate logout, cart mutation, or analytics-heavy routes.
Prerender can run page code earlier than a user expects.
Rules should respect authentication and privacy boundaries.
accessibility: Speculation must not change focus, announce content, or mutate state before navigation.
Keep navigation links usable without speculation support.
Avoid preloading sensitive pages for shared devices.

name: datalist
support: datalist is a long-standing form feature, though rendering and filtering details vary by browser and input type.
summary: Offer native suggestions for an input while still allowing values outside the suggestion list.
solves: Search boxes, airport codes, saved filters, and administrative inputs where suggestions help but should not constrain the user.
syntax:
<label for="airport">Airport</label>
<input id="airport" name="airport" list="airport-options">
<datalist id="airport-options">
  <option value="SFO"></option>
  <option value="HND"></option>
</datalist>
demo: A route planner input that suggests airport codes while allowing custom charter stops.
usage: Use when suggestions are helpful but not exclusive. Use select when the user must choose one of a fixed set.
pitfalls: Styling the popup is intentionally limited.
Some input types have uneven datalist support.
Suggestions are not validation.
accessibility: Keep a visible label on the input.
Do not rely on placeholder text as the only instruction.
Validate free-form values when the backend has constraints.

name: form attribute
support: The form owner attribute is widely supported for associating controls with forms outside their DOM ancestor.
summary: Associate controls with a form by id even when layout places them outside the form element.
solves: Sticky toolbars, split layouts, tables, and modals where submit buttons or inputs cannot live inside the form markup.
syntax:
<form id="profile-form"></form>
<input name="displayName" form="profile-form">
<button type="submit" form="profile-form">Save profile</button>
demo: A split profile editor where a sticky footer button submits the form without wrapping the whole layout.
usage: Use when layout structure and form ownership need to differ. Keep the relationship obvious in code with stable ids.
pitfalls: The target form id must be stable and unique.
Not every custom abstraction forwards form attributes correctly.
The visual grouping can mislead users if labels and sections are unclear.
accessibility: Keep related controls visually grouped and labeled.
Make submit buttons clearly describe which form they submit.
Do not separate controls so far that keyboard users lose context.

name: Submitter overrides
support: Submitter attributes such as formaction, formmethod, formenctype, formtarget, and formnovalidate are widely supported.
summary: Let individual submit buttons override where and how a form submits.
solves: Save draft, publish, preview, export, and destructive confirmation flows that share fields but differ in submission intent.
syntax:
<form action="/publish" method="post">
  <button type="submit" formaction="/drafts" formnovalidate>Save draft</button>
  <button type="submit">Publish</button>
</form>
demo: A CMS action bar where Save draft skips validation while Publish keeps full validation.
usage: Use when one set of fields has multiple legitimate submit destinations or validation modes.
pitfalls: Only submit buttons can use submitter overrides.
Server-side routing must still verify user intent.
formnovalidate should not bypass required checks for final submission.
accessibility: Button labels must state the outcome.
Warn before irreversible actions.
Preserve validation messages for final submit paths.

name: Constraint validation
support: HTML constraint validation is widely supported for native form controls and provides browser-owned validity states.
summary: Use required, pattern, minlength, min, max, step, and related constraints before writing custom validators.
solves: Forms that need immediate, native validation feedback without rebuilding basic validity state from scratch.
syntax:
<input name="repo" required pattern="[a-z0-9-]+" minlength="3">
<input name="stars" type="number" min="0" step="1">
<button>Save</button>
demo: A repository settings form that shows which constraints are native and which remain product rules.
usage: Use native constraints for syntax and presence checks, then layer product-specific validation on top.
pitfalls: pattern validates full values, not partial matches.
Native messages vary by browser and locale.
Client validation never replaces server validation.
accessibility: Associate errors with fields.
Do not rely on color alone for invalid state.
Keep instructions visible before the user submits.

name: Input hints
support: inputmode and enterkeyhint are widely available and especially useful on virtual keyboards.
summary: Hint the right mobile keyboard and enter-key action without changing the semantic input type.
solves: OTP, price, search, and chat inputs where keyboard ergonomics matter but validation semantics are separate.
syntax:
<input autocomplete="one-time-code" inputmode="numeric" enterkeyhint="done" maxlength="6">
<input type="search" enterkeyhint="search" autocomplete="off">
demo: A sign-in code form that uses numeric keyboard hints and one-time-code autocomplete.
usage: Use inputmode for keyboard shape, type for data semantics, and autocomplete tokens for autofill intent.
pitfalls: inputmode does not validate the value.
Virtual keyboard behavior varies by platform.
autocomplete tokens must match real field purpose.
accessibility: Keep visible labels and instructions.
Do not hide paste support for one-time codes.
Use autocomplete responsibly for personal data.

name: download attribute
support: The download attribute is widely available, with same-origin and browser policy limits that can affect whether it is honored.
summary: Mark a link as a download and provide a suggested filename without JavaScript.
solves: Export buttons, generated reports, invoices, CSV templates, and local Blob downloads.
demo: A report export link that names the file and keeps navigation behavior clear.
usage: Use for same-origin files or generated Blob URLs where a download is the user's intended action.
pitfalls: Cross-origin downloads may ignore the suggested filename.
The browser can adjust invalid filenames.
download is a hint, not a permission bypass.
accessibility: Link text should say what file type or result will download.
Expose file size when it is significant.
Do not surprise users by downloading from ordinary navigation links.

name: theme-color
support: theme-color is useful for browser chrome and installed experiences, but support and exact UI effects vary by browser and platform.
summary: Suggest colors for the browser or surrounding UI using meta tags, optionally scoped by media query.
solves: Mobile browser chrome, installed web app polish, and dark/light brand alignment outside the page canvas.
syntax:
<meta name="theme-color" content="#ffffff" media="(prefers-color-scheme: light)">
<meta name="theme-color" content="#0f172a" media="(prefers-color-scheme: dark)">
demo: A brand chrome palette that pairs page theme tokens with browser UI theme-color tags.
usage: Use one or more meta tags in the head. Add media queries when the page supports multiple color schemes.
pitfalls: Some desktop browsers parse it but do not visibly use it.
Platform UI can override or transform the color.
The tag should follow theme changes if the user can switch themes.
accessibility: Choose colors with sufficient contrast against browser UI icons where applicable.
Keep page content contrast independent from browser chrome.
Do not rely on chrome color as the only state indicator.

name: color-scheme meta
support: meta name=color-scheme is broadly supported and works with the browser's form controls, scrollbars, and default colors.
summary: Tell the browser which color schemes the document supports before CSS has fully loaded.
solves: Dark mode pages where native controls and UA surfaces should not flash with the wrong default scheme.
demo: A settings page head snippet that aligns native controls with the page's dark and light modes.
usage: Use in the head when the whole document supports light, dark, or both. Pair with CSS color-scheme for component-level control.
pitfalls: This does not create a complete theme by itself.
Order matters when declaring preference.
Some embedded content may have its own scheme.
accessibility: Respect user color-scheme preferences.
Maintain contrast in both schemes.
Test native controls, scrollbars, and selection colors.

name: iframe sandbox
support: The iframe sandbox attribute is widely available and provides a browser-enforced restriction set for embedded content.
summary: Put embedded documents in a restricted environment, then opt back into only the capabilities they need.
solves: User-generated previews, third-party widgets, ad slots, and internal tools that should not inherit full page power.
demo: A user-content preview frame with explicit capability tokens instead of full trust.
usage: Start with an empty sandbox, then add the smallest set of allow tokens that makes the embed work.
pitfalls: allow-scripts plus allow-same-origin can let same-origin embeds remove sandboxing.
Sandboxing can break forms, popups, downloads, and storage unless allowed.
A permissive token list can become security theater.
accessibility: Every iframe needs a useful title.
Embedded content still needs keyboard and screen reader support.
Provide a fallback or link when embedded content cannot load.

name: iframe srcdoc
support: srcdoc is widely available for embedding inline HTML into an iframe, with security considerations for untrusted strings.
summary: Create a self-contained iframe document from inline HTML markup.
solves: Live previews, email template sandboxes, component docs, and generated snippets that should render in an isolated document.
demo: An email preview frame that renders generated markup without writing a temporary route.
usage: Use with sandbox for untrusted or user-authored markup. Prefer TrustedHTML when assigning srcdoc from script in strict environments.
pitfalls: Untrusted srcdoc content can still be dangerous without sandboxing.
Escaping inline HTML quickly becomes hard to read.
Large srcdoc documents are harder to cache than URL-based embeds.
accessibility: Give the iframe a descriptive title.
Ensure the embedded document has meaningful headings.
Avoid trapping keyboard users inside preview frames.

name: iframe credentialless
support: credentialless iframes are experimental and currently best treated as a COEP-oriented progressive enhancement.
summary: Load an iframe into an ephemeral context without the embedded origin's normal cookies, storage, or credentials.
solves: COEP pages that need to embed third-party content that does not itself opt into cross-origin isolation.
demo: A cross-origin widget embed plan that separates credentialless isolation from classic sandboxing.
usage: Use only when you understand the cross-origin isolation requirement and the embed can function without its normal credentials.
pitfalls: The embedded page sees fresh ephemeral storage.
Login-dependent widgets may break.
Support is not broad enough for required functionality.
accessibility: Provide a fallback link for unsupported browsers.
Keep the iframe title clear about the third-party content.
Do not hide critical workflow steps in an optional credentialless embed.
